title: Rabi-coupling-induced three-component quantum droplet in ultracold Bose gases
authors: Xiao Ding, Dajun Wang, Xiaoling Cui
abstract: We uncover a new mechanism for realizing three-component quantum droplets in ultracold Bose gases, where only one inter-species interaction is attractive. In this scheme, the inter-species attraction leads to a self-bound binary droplet, and the third component joins through Rabi coupling with one component of the binary droplet. We find that a stronger Rabi coupling leads to a larger fraction of the third component, but also destabilizes the entire droplet due to the involvement of more repulsive forces. Such instability can be remedied by a finite detuning between the Rabi-coupled components. We demonstrate these results in realistic Na-Rb mixtures, using both thermodynamic analyses and numerical simulations based on extended Gross-Pitaevskii equations. Our work outlines a general route for stabilizing multi-component droplets by bridging an existing binary droplet with additional components via suitable single-particle fields.
summary: This paper proposes a method to create three-component quantum droplets by using Rabi coupling to attach a third species to an existing binary droplet. It identifies how Rabi coupling and detuning compete to either expand or destabilize the droplet. This work offers a new strategy for stabilizing multi-component quantum liquids in ultracold atomic mixtures.
main_problem: The study investigates a new mechanism to realize three-component quantum droplets in ultracold Bose gases where only one inter-species interaction is attractive, addressing the challenge of stabilizing such droplets against repulsive forces.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that Rabi coupling can bind a third component to a binary droplet, and that while strong coupling increases the third component's fraction, it can be stabilized using finite magnetic detuning.
method: The research employs mean-field analysis, Bogoliubov theory to incorporate Lee-Huang-Yang corrections, thermodynamic analysis in the thermodynamic limit, and numerical simulations using extended Gross-Pitaevskii equations.
model_or_system: A three-component mixture of ultracold Bose gases, specifically a realistic Na-Rb system, where components 1 and 2 form a binary droplet and component 3 is coupled via Rabi coupling and detuning.
key_observables: Chemical potentials, density profiles (flat-top structure), density ratio (n3/n2), spin polarization, and stability regimes (Collapse-I and Collapse-II).
important_parameters: Rabi coupling strength (Omega), magnetic detuning (delta), inter-species/intra-species interaction strengths (g_ij), and scattering lengths (a_ij).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes spherical symmetry for the droplet configuration and is performed in the thermodynamic limit before being extended to finite sizes via GPE.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows a schematic of the coupling mechanism; Fig 3 plots droplet solutions and stability regions in the density plane; Fig 4 shows the density ratio as a function of Rabi coupling; Fig 5 displays the numerical flat-top density profiles.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the mechanism, presents the theoretical model and Hamiltonian, details the thermodynamic and Bogoliubov stability analysis, provides numerical verification via GPE simulations, and discusses the implications for multi-component droplet stabilization.
why_interesting: It provides a general route for engineering multi-component self-bound states using single-particle fields (Rabi coupling), which is highly relevant for controlling many-body dynamics and creating complex quantum fluids in AMO physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustration for achieving a three-component (1,2,3) quantum droplet. Components 1 and 2 form a binary droplet in vacuum due to inter-species attraction, and component 3 joins via coherent Rabi coupling with component 2, thereby facilitating the formation of a (1,2,3) droplet. The coupling constants in all other interaction channels are repulsive.

FIG. 2. Mean-field phase diagram of three-component bosons in (n1, n23) plane. Three phases are identified: a sta- ble miscible phase (‘stable’), and two unstable phases towards collapse of component-1 (‘collapse-I’) and collapse of all com- ponents (‘collapse-II’). Here we take the Na-Rb mixture at δ = 0, and the densities are scaled by Ω/g11.

FIG. 3. Quantum droplet solutions in the (n1, n23) plane at fixed δ = 0 and varying Ω/(ℏν0) = 0.2 (a), 0.8 (b), and 1.4 (c), with ν0 = 20 kHz. The black dashed curve shows the zero-pressure contour, while the blue and green dash-dotted curves denote the locations where the chemical potentials meet the vacuum values: µα = µ(0) α . The shaded region marks the regime where Eq. (13) is satisfied. The red solid dot indicates the stable droplet solution on the zero-pressure contour. The red hollow dot marks the global minimum on the zero-pressure contour when it lies outside the stable region. In these plots, stable droplets exist in (a,b) but not in (c). The density unit is ℏν0/g11, and the...

Fig. 3(b)). Nevertheless, the zero-pressure contour can still cross the shaded area, and one can identify the stable solution as the lowest-energy point on the contour within the area, as shown by the solid red dot in Fig. 3(b). Upon further increasing Ω, the shaded area disappears and a stable droplet solution can no longer be supported (see Fig. 3(c)).

FIG. 4. Density ratio n3/n2 as a function of Ωat different de- tunings δ = 0, 0.8, 1.2, 1.6, 2.0. The triangles mark the upper bounds of Ωand n3/n2 for a stable three-component droplet. The units of Ωand δ are ℏν0, with ν0 = 20 kHz. Other pa- rameters are the same as specified in the main text for the Na-Rb system.
